source_metadata.title_raw: Sarah J. Knecht - A-19-009 - February 14, 2019 - Santa Barbara

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Sarah J. Knecht 
City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
740 State Street, Suite #201 
Santa Barbara, CA 93101 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-009 
Dear Ms. Knecht: 
February 14, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions 
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May City Councilmember Randy Rowse take part in governmental decisions related to the 
proposed development of a new police station and relocation of a weekly farmer's market, given his 
business entity and leasehold interests related to his nearby restaurant? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The governmental decisions at issue would have reasonably foreseeable, material 
financial effects on Councilmember Rowse's business entity and leasehold interests relating to his 
restaurant, as the decisions would likely affect the amount of business the restaurant receives. 2 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Please note that when a public official who holds an office specified in Section 87200, including a city 
councilmember, has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19-009 
Page No. 2 
The City of Santa Barbara is proposing to construct a new police station and related parking 
facilities. The City is in the site selection phase. Once a site is selected, the ideal new facility would 
include a 60,850 square-foot building, 252 on-site secure parking spaces, and 80 public parking 
spaces that could be located off-site. The current police station has approximately 270 employees, 
40-50 of which may be on-site at any time. 
The City's top choice for the site of the new facility is the "Cota Lot," which encompasses 
60,616 square-feet, is located approximately half a mile from the existing police station and is 
currently a commuter parking lot. Approximately 400 commuters have parking passes to use the lot, 
but "only a percentage use it at any one time." Commuters currently using the Cota Lot are 
downtown workers with offices nearby. If the Cota Lot is selected for the new police station, the 
commuters using the lot will need to find alternate parking. Some of the parkers would be relocated 
to a City-owned public parking structure on Anacapa Street. It is currently unclear where all of the 
alternate parking sites would be located. However, adjacent to the Cota Lot is a parcel of property 
owned by the State of California, a part of which the City may consider purchasing or leasing for 
additional on-site public parking. 
The Cota Lot is also the current home of the Saturday Farmer's Market. If the Cota Lot is 
selected as the site for the new police station, the Saturday Farmer's Market could be relocated. A 
City-owned parcel, known as De La Guerra Plaza, is being considered as a relocation site for the 
Farmer's Market. De La Guerra Plaza is located adjacent to, and shares the same parcel as, Santa 
Barbara City Hall at 735 Anacapa Street, approximately 0.3 miles from the Cota Lot. 
Councilmember Rowse is the sole owner of The Paradise Cafe, a restaurant that occupies a 
leasehold at 702 Anacapa Street. His financial stake in the business and leasehold each exceed 
$2,000 in value. The real property leased by Councilmember Rowse is located approximately 286 
feet from the Cota Lot and 220 feet from the State-owned property. It is also located 179 feet from 
the City Hall property, which includes De La Guerra Plaza. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or using his 
or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. 
Interests 
Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one or more of the official's interests. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise 
' 
(1) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type of economic interest involved in the 
decision as well as details of the economic interest on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) 
leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. (Section 87105; Regulation 18707.) 


File No. A-19-009 
Page No. 3 
are defined in Section 87103, as pertinent to the facts provided potentially disqualifying interest and 
include the following: 
• 
A public official's own finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate family. 
• 
Any business entity in which t)1e public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two 
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. 
• 
Any source of income aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or 
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. 
• 
Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
• 
Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating to $500 or more in value received by, or provided 
to, or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the decision. 
(Section 87103(a)-(e).) 
Based on the facts provided, Councilmember Rowse has several economic interests related 
to the Paradise Cafe: (1) an economic interest in the Paradise Cafe as a business entity in which he 
has an investment worth $2,000 or more; (2) a source-of-income interest in the business; and (3) a 
leasehold interest in the property where the restaurant is located. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the 
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmentaJ decision before the official 
or the official's agency. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Regarding financial interests not explicitly involved 
in a decision, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In 
general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical 
or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent 
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably 
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) 
· Generally, where a governmental decision will have a financial effect on a business entity 
not expressly involved in that decision, the materiality of the financial effect is analyzed under 
Regulation 18702.1 (b ). 3 That regulation states: "the financial effect is material if a prudent person 
with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial 
effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the 
value of a privately-held business entity." Examples of potentially applicable decisions include 
those that: 
• 
Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business entity supplies; 
3 Regulation 18702.1 also governs the materiality of financial effects on a source of income where the source is a 
business entity. (Regulation 18702.3(a)(4).) 


FileNo. A-19-009 
Page No. 4 
• 
Make improvements in the surrounding neighborhood such as redevelopment projects, 
traffic/road improvements, or parking changes that may affect, either temporarily or 
permanently, the amount of business the business entity receives; or 
• 
Decide the location of a major development, entertainment facility, or other project that 
would increase or decrease the amount of business the entity draws from the location of the 
project. 
(Regulation 18702.1 (b )(3)-(5).) 
The reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a governmental decision on any real property 
in which a governmental official has a leasehold financial interest is material whenever the 
governmental decision will: 
• 
Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; 
• 
Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property. 
(Regulation 18702.2(c)(2), (4).) 
A governmental decision that would determine whether a new police station would be 
constructed less than 300 feet away from the Paradise Cafe would have a reasonably foreseeable 
financial effect on the restaurant. Due to the close proximity, construction would affect nearby 
traffic, parking accessibility, and the number of potential restaurant customers, particularly while 
the Cota Lot has neither commuter parkers nor police station employees. Even after the construction 
is done, there may be a significant difference in the number of customers the Paradise Cafe receives 
as a result of the new police station, compared to the number of customers it received from the 
commuter lot. The characteristics of a police station may also have a significant impact on nearby 
businesses including possible effects on noise level and the character of the neighborhood. 
Accordingly, it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decisions regarding the 
development of a new police would a material financial effect on Councilmember Rowse' s interests 
in his business entity. 
With respect to the Farmer's Market, decisions to relocate the market to another location 
further away than the Cota Lot could potentially result in the restaurant losing customers on market 
days thereby affecting the value of the company. Accordingly, it is also reasonably foreseeable that 
any decision to relocate the Farmer's market will have a material financial effect on 
Councilmember Rowse's interests in.his business entity. 
Councilmember Rowse has disqualifying financial interests because governmental decisions 
relating to the development of a new police station and the relocation of the nearby Farmer's 
Market would have reasonably foreseeable, material financial effects on his interests. Consequently, 
under the PRA, he must recuse himself and not take part in these governmental decisions. 


File No. A-19-009 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:gal 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division
